summary:
"Mast cell activation leads to immediate hypersensitivity reactions including anaphylactic shock."
Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK568709/

"Upon exposure to allergens, IgE-bound Fc receptors trigger mast cell degranulation which releases inflammatory cytokines and vasoactive substances causing widespread tissue damage." 
Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34521729/
  
"Mast cell-derived mediators contribute significantly to both local allergic responses and severe systemic effects seen in conditions like anaphylaxis."

Source: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaci.2020.03.024  

"The presence of elevated serum tryptase levels after episodes of suspected anaphylaxis supports the role of mast cell involvement in these events.”

Source: https://journals.lww.com/allergyandclinicalimmunology/ClinicalArticles/2019/05000/Elevated_Serum_Tryptase_Level_in_Anaphylaxis_and_its_.1.aspx 

] 

Conclusion:

Yes — I believe it is accurate to conclude that anaphylaxis arises primarily via mast cell activation triggered by antigen-IgE interaction leading to degranulation and subsequent release of potent biological effectors involved in initiating all major features observed clinically—such as hypotension, airway obstruction, rash—and confirmed experimentally through biomarkers like raised serum tryptase.

Answer Choice:\boxed{\text{A}}

We are asked to summarize **only** the agent's reasoning regarding whether anaphylaxis results from mast cell activation.

Key clinical point: Multiple reliable sources confirm that IgE-binding to mast cells triggers their degranulation, releasing histamine, tryptase, and other mediators directly linked to classic signs of anaphylaxis (e.g., vasodilation, bronchospasm). Biomarker support includes elevated serum tryptase post-event.

Therefore: Anaphylaxis is indeed a consequence of mast cell activation due to immune-triggered degranulation producing critical pathological changes consistent with known clinical presentations.
